skill.name: npm-helper
skill.description: NPM and Node.js package management guidance for installs, project setup, dependency troubleshooting, security checks, and package publishing workflows.
skill.summary: Manage Node.js packages, dependency health, and package manager workflows.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many external command, filesystem, network, and sensitive-file patterns in SKILL.md. Most are legitimate npm documentation examples, but the skill also recommends high-impact operations such as recursive sudo ownership changes, hidden shell configuration edits, and package publishing commands. No evidence found of prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Recursive sudo ownership changes in remediation guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill advises recursive sudo ownership changes for npm permission errors. These commands can alter user and system package directories and can cause privilege or integrity problems if followed automatically.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain sudo commands with command substitution and recursive ownership changes. The context is troubleshooting, not malware, but the operational risk is direct and high impact.

medium_findings.title: Broad external package manager command execution guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill provides many npm, yarn, pnpm, npx, and shell commands. This is expected for the skill, but an agent could install packages, modify lock files, run scripts, or publish packages without careful user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, and several can change local projects or registry state. They match the skill purpose, so this is a legitimate-use risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Hidden configuration and sensitive file guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes examples that append to .npmrc, .yarnrc.yml, and shell startup files, and it discusses .env files. These files can contain credentials or affect future shell and package manager behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly create or append to hidden configuration files and mention environment files. The examples are common in npm workflows, but they require user review because they can persist sensitive or security-relevant settings.

medium_findings.title: Network-dependent package and audit workflows
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill recommends package installs, npx-based security tooling, and npm registry configuration. These workflows contact external package services and can execute downloaded tooling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network use is inherent to npm package management and registry access. I found no evidence of exfiltration, but the external dependency and execution surface is real.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography detections correspond to ordinary words or package configuration terms, such as description fields, overrides, and semantic versioning. No cryptographic algorithm use was found in those contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review showed package metadata and configuration examples, not hashing or encryption calls. The scanner likely matched substrings inside normal npm terminology.

low_findings.title: Network scanning detection is a false positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The detected line is a TypeScript compiler source map option. It is not a network scanner or reconnaissance command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line appears inside tsconfig.json content and contains declarationMap. There is no command invocation or network scanning tool in that context.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo with recursive ownership changes
dangerous_patterns.description: The guidance includes sudo chown commands that recursively change ownership of npm and system package directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and can modify sensitive local directories. Their risk is confirmed even though the surrounding section is troubleshooting guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent shell configuration modification
dangerous_patterns.description: The guidance appends a PATH export to a shell startup file and sources it in the current shell.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly modify and source a hidden shell configuration file. This can persist behavior beyond the immediate task.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package publishing commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The guidance includes npm version and publish commands that can change package metadata and publish artifacts to a registry.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The publishing commands are explicit and have external side effects. They are legitimate for an npm helper but require user confirmation and credential awareness.

content.value_statement: Node projects often suffer from dependency drift, install failures, and unclear package manager choices. This skill guides package setup, troubleshooting, auditing, migration, and publishing workflows.
actual_capabilities: Compare npm, Yarn, and pnpm commands for common package workflows.
Guide Node.js project initialization and package.json configuration.
Troubleshoot dependency conflicts, peer dependency warnings, and install failures.
Recommend security audit and dependency update workflows.
Explain monorepo workspace commands for npm, Yarn, and pnpm.
Outline package publishing steps, registry settings, and release checks.
limitations: It does not verify package safety before installation unless the agent performs extra checks.
Some examples can modify local configuration files or package registry state.
It cannot guarantee compatibility across every Node.js version or operating system.
Publishing and permission fixes require explicit user review before execution.

use_cases.title: Start a Node.js project
use_cases.description: Create a package manager plan, choose dependencies, and set up scripts for a new application.

use_cases.title: Repair dependency problems
use_cases.description: Diagnose peer dependency warnings, corrupted installs, cache problems, and version conflicts.

use_cases.title: Prepare a package release
use_cases.description: Review publish settings, package contents, version changes, and registry configuration before release.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a package manager
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose npm, Yarn, or pnpm for this Node.js project. Explain the tradeoffs and safest setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Fix install failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my dependency install error and propose a step-by-step fix. Ask before suggesting destructive cleanup commands.

prompt_templates.title: Audit dependency health
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this package.json for outdated, risky, or unnecessary dependencies. Recommend safe update and audit actions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan package publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release checklist for publishing this npm package. Include registry, files, versioning, testing, and rollback checks.

output_examples.input: I need to set up a new TypeScript package.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan with package manager choice, dependency groups, script names, TypeScript configuration goals, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: My install fails because of peer dependency conflicts.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting sequence that identifies the conflicting packages, checks compatible versions, and avoids broad force installs when possible.

output_examples.input: I am ready to publish my package.
output_examples.output: A release checklist covering authentication, package contents, version changes, dry-run review, access level, and post-publish verification.

best_practices: Review every command before execution, especially commands that change files, install packages, or publish releases.
Commit lock files and use reproducible install modes in continuous integration.
Run dependency audits and inspect major version updates before applying them.
anti_patterns: Do not run recursive permission changes without understanding the affected directories.
Do not publish packages or change registry settings without explicit release approval.
Do not bypass peer dependency checks as a permanent fix for incompatible packages.

faq.question: Can this skill install npm packages?
faq.answer: It can guide an agent through install commands, but users should approve each command before execution.

faq.question: Does it support Yarn and pnpm?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes command comparisons and migration guidance for npm, Yarn, and pnpm.

faq.question: Can it help with security audits?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains npm audit, dependency updates, and third-party audit tooling workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe for automated use?
faq.answer: No. Some guidance can modify files, install tools, or publish packages, so human confirmation is needed.

faq.question: Can it fix peer dependency errors?
faq.answer: It can propose diagnosis and resolution steps, including version alignment and cautious fallback options.

faq.question: Does it handle package publishing?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides publishing workflow guidance, but release commands require explicit approval and credential checks.
